title: The Heyday
summary: The Rat ruled Kenmore Square, WBCN owned the airwaves, and the Dropkick Murphys formed in a Quincy barbershop basement. The Telecommunications Act of 1996 began the slow corporate takeover of radio that would eventually gut local music coverage. But for now, the scene was electric — Letters to Cleo, Mighty Mighty Bosstones, and Morphine were all in their prime.

title: The Transition
summary: The Rat closed in '97. Mark Sandman died onstage in '99. The old guard was fading, but something new was taking root — DIY house shows filled the gaps left by shrinking venues, the Dresden Dolls invented 'Brechtian punk cabaret' in a Boston loft, and Great Scott started its transformation into an every-night live music room in Allston.

title: The Unraveling Begins
summary: Dropkick Murphys' 'I'm Shipping Up to Boston' exploded via Scorsese's The Departed and became the sound of Boston sports. But beneath the surface, the infrastructure was crumbling. WFNX got a signal boost in 2006, buying time, while Passion Pit formed at Emerson College — proof the scene still had creative fire even as the ground shifted.

title: The Death of Radio
summary: WBCN signed off on August 11, 2009, after 41 years — its last song was Pink Floyd's 'Shine On You Crazy Diamond.' Three years later, WFNX was sold to Clear Channel for $14.5 million, its last song The Cure's 'Let's Go to Bed.' Billy Ruane — the man who helped build the Middle East into a music venue — died in October 2010. The connective tissue was tearing.

title: Last One Standing
summary: The Boston Phoenix published its final issue in March 2013 — 47 years of music listings, gone. The Rat was gone. WBCN was gone. WFNX was gone. BostonBands was the last one standing. Then the Marathon bombing shook the city. Six weeks later, Boston Calling launched at City Hall Plaza — a cathartic moment from the ashes.

title: The Archive Opens
summary: In late 2016, BostonBands began importing gig data from external sources and the archive exploded. Monthly gig counts jumped from dozens to over a thousand. Boston Calling moved to the Harvard Athletic Complex. For three years the data painted a portrait of the full Boston music ecosystem.

title: The Silence
summary: COVID-19 shut down live music in March 2020. Great Scott — 44 years in Allston — closed permanently. McGreevy's shuttered. Boston Calling was canceled. The silence was deafening, and for many venues and musicians, it was permanent.

title: Rebuilding
summary: Roadrunner opened in Brighton — 3,500 capacity, named after Jonathan Richman's 'Roadrunner.' Boston Calling returned with a stage for local talent. MGM Music Hall opened at Fenway. The scene wasn't what it was — but it was alive.

title: The Archive
summary: Thirty years after that first graffiti wall went live, the curtain comes down — but the music doesn't disappear. This site is the archive, a place to preserve what BostonBands built: the bands, the venues, the songs, and the memories.
